my_sentence: I read some news about AI company.
correction: I read some news about an AI company.
explanation: When you talk about one, countable, singular thing, you must use an article. 'AI company' is a singular countable noun, so it needs 'an' (or 'the' if specific); leaving it bare is wrong. If you mean more than one, use the plural ('AI companies').
correct_examples: I read an article about an AI company.
There are many AI companies in the world.
context: Talking about what Jack did during the day (podcasts, AI companies)

my_sentence: I have some free time during my work, so I'm not busy.
correction: I have some free time at work, so I'm not busy.
explanation: Use 'at work' to mean 'while at one's job'. 'during my work' is an L1-style literal translation and sounds unnatural in English.
correct_examples: I have some free time at work.
She reads emails at work in the morning.
context: Saying he was not very busy that day

my_sentence: You can use them to your work and improve your efficiency.
correction: You can use them at work to improve your efficiency.
explanation: 'use something at work' is the correct collocation for applying a skill in your job. 'to your work' is the wrong preposition here.
correct_examples: You can use these skills at work.
I apply what I learn at work every day.
context: Explaining how training helps you keep a job

my_sentence: Many years later you can become an expert in your industry.
correction: Many years later you can become an expert in your career.
explanation: Here you mean the field of work you personally build over time, so 'career' fits better than 'industry'. 'industry' refers to a whole economic sector, not your individual professional path.
correct_examples: After ten years she became an expert in her career.
He built a successful career in software.
context: Explaining how learning skills helps you keep a job

my_sentence: If someone can arrive on time, he did all her tasks on time, so we can arrange tasks to him.
correction: If someone can finish his or her tasks on time, he or she can be responsible for his or her tasks.
explanation: When the subject is 'someone', keep the pronouns consistent: use 'he or she' / 'his or her' throughout, not a mix of 'he', 'her', 'him'. Don't switch gender or person within the same sentence.
correct_examples: If someone finishes his or her work on time, he or she is reliable.
Everyone should bring his or her own laptop.
context: Listing strategies for keeping a job (arriving on time)

my_sentence: Keeping a good attitude is useful for keeping a job.
correction: Keeping a good attitude is useful for having a job.
explanation: Avoid repeating the same word ('keeping ... keeping') in one sentence. A gerund like 'keeping' usually has no exact synonym, so the repetition is awkward; replace the second one with 'having a job'.
correct_examples: Keeping a good attitude is useful for having a job.
Staying calm is important for doing good work.
context: Writing strategies for keeping a job

my_sentence: It can help you keep professional in front of your customers.
correction: It can help you be professional in front of your customers.
explanation: The natural collocation is 'be professional', not 'keep professional'. ('keep' also repeats the earlier 'keeping', which the teacher was avoiding.)
correct_examples: A good attitude helps you be professional with clients.
Try to be professional in meetings.
context: Explaining why a good attitude helps at work

my_sentence: Aside from it, asking questions is useful... When you join a new group you will meet a lot of problems and it can help you a lot.
correction: Aside from that, asking questions is useful... When you join a new group you will meet a lot of problems and that can help you a lot.
explanation: Use the demonstrative pronoun 'that' (not 'it') when you refer back to a whole idea or the entire situation you just described. 'it' points to one specific noun; 'that' wraps up everything previously said.
correct_examples: I lost my keys, and that ruined my morning.
Aside from that, we should also test the code.
context: Listing strategies for keeping a job (asking questions)

my_sentence: For some functions that AI can replace, and for other...
correction: For some functions that AI can replace, it can help you navigate and find your items.
explanation: 'For some functions that AI can replace' is only a fragment (a prepositional phrase + relative clause), not a complete sentence. After the comma you must add a complete independent clause with its own subject and verb to finish the idea.
correct_examples: For the tasks that AI can handle, it works faster than people.
In the cases that AI can solve, it saves a lot of time.
context: Discussing which functions AI can replace in a store

my_sentence: AI can use their camera to take some photos.
correction: AI can use its camera to take some photos.
explanation: 'AI' is singular and not a person, so the possessive is 'its', not 'their'. Match the pronoun to the singular, non-human subject.
correct_examples: The robot uses its camera to scan items.
The system saves its data automatically.
context: Explaining how AI checks item quality with photos

my_sentence: We need to through the bad meat into the garbage.
correction: We need to throw the bad meat into the garbage.
explanation: 'throw' (toss away, /θroʊ/) and 'through' (passing across, /θruː/) sound and spell differently and have different meanings. You wanted 'throw away', so use 'throw'.
correct_examples: Please throw the trash in the bin.
We walked through the park.
context: Talking about AI checking food/item quality

my_sentence: We have our own model to help us finish a specific scenario.
correction: We have our own model to help us finish a specific task.
explanation: What the model completes is a 'task' (a specific job/operation), not a 'scenario' (a situation or scene). Use 'task' for something to be done.
correct_examples: We trained a model for a specific task.
The tool automates repetitive tasks.
context: Explaining the company's own AI model

my_sentence: Nowadays some country try to use AI technology to solve cases.
correction: Nowadays some countries try to use AI technology to solve cases.
explanation: 'some' with a countable noun takes the plural form: 'some countries'. The verb 'try' is then correct for the plural subject.
correct_examples: Some countries have banned the app.
Some companies use AI to screen resumes.
context: Talking about countries using AI to solve criminal cases

my_sentence: He or she just needs to take a breath and think a little.
correction: He or she just needs to calm down and think about their behavior a little.
explanation: To describe controlling emotions, 'calm down' (or 'take a breather') is the natural phrase. 'take a breath' is literal (one inhale) and doesn't carry the meaning of settling your mood.
correct_examples: When you get angry, calm down before you reply.
Take a breather and think it over.
context: How someone keeps a good attitude toward others

my_sentence: Showing a good manner is a good attitude.
correction: Showing good manners is a good attitude.
explanation: In the sense of polite social behavior, 'manners' is always plural and takes no article: 'good manners'. The singular 'a manner' means 'a way of doing something', which is not what you mean here.
correct_examples: Children should learn good manners.
He always shows good manners at the table.
context: Discussing manners as part of a good attitude

my_sentence: If you speak, you will choke.
correction: If you speak, you can choke.
explanation: Use 'can' for possibility, not 'will'. Speaking with a full mouth doesn't guarantee choking; it only makes it possible, so 'can choke' is correct.
correct_examples: If you eat too fast, you can choke.
If you touch it, you can get burned.
context: Explaining table manners (not talking with a full mouth)

my_sentence: If you point out it in public, he or she will hate you.
correction: If you point it out in public, he or she will hate you.
explanation: With a separable phrasal verb like 'point out', a pronoun object must go between the verb and the particle: 'point it out', never 'point out it'. (A full noun can go either side: 'point out the mistake' / 'point the mistake out'.)
correct_examples: Don't point it out in front of everyone.
She turned it off before leaving.
context: Talking about correcting someone's behavior privately vs. publicly

my_sentence: Even though you respect for them and you call them older, they will feel angry.
correction: Even though you respect them and you call them older, they will feel angry.
explanation: The verb 'respect' takes a direct object with no preposition: 'respect them', not 'respect for them'. (The noun 'respect for someone' does take 'for', but the verb does not.)
correct_examples: I respect my teachers.
They respect each other's opinions.
context: Discussing how addressing women as 'older' can offend

my_sentence: We can ask many questions for free from them.
correction: We can ask many questions freely from them.
explanation: To mean 'without hesitation / openly', use the adverb 'freely' to modify the verb 'ask'. 'for free' means 'without paying', which is not what you mean here.
correct_examples: You can ask me anything freely.
Speak freely; no one will judge you.
context: Explaining how building relationships lets you ask questions

my_sentence: We need to find the core members for a project, because there are too many people, we cannot build a good connection with them.
correction: Since there are too many people, we cannot build a good connection with them.
explanation: When the reason clause comes first, 'Since ...' is the natural linker, followed by a comma and the main clause. Putting 'because' mid-sentence here created a run-on; lead with 'Since' to give the cause cleanly.
correct_examples: Since there are too many people, we can't talk to everyone.
Since it was raining, we stayed inside.
context: Explaining why you must find the core members of a project

my_sentence: Different projects will have opposing groups.
correction: Different projects will not have similar groups.
explanation: 'opposing' means 'against / in conflict', which reverses your meaning. You meant that each project has its own different team, so say the projects 'will not have similar groups'.
correct_examples: Different projects will not have the same team.
Each project has its own group of people.
context: Explaining that each project has its own separate team

my_sentence: I can write a little but can't write a lot.
correction: I can write a little, but not a lot.
explanation: Don't repeat 'write'. Once the verb is established, drop the second 'can't write' and use the concise contrast 'but not a lot'.
correct_examples: I can walk a little, but not a lot.
She speaks some French, but not fluently.
context: Telling the teacher about his recovering hand
